title: Macramé round rug
subtitle: A flat knotted circle worked from the centre in 8 mm braided cotton
excerpt: A 60 cm diameter flat circular rug worked outward from a central ring using alternating square knots in 8 mm braided cotton. The circular construction requires adding working cords as the radius grows to keep the piece flat. Finished with a wrapped edge and trimmed ends. Durable enough for light foot traffic and can be hand-washed.
sourceNotes:
- General macramé practice: circular flat construction, cord increase method
- Braided cotton rug construction: edge finishing, cord management at scale

glossaryTerms.definition: A symmetrical flat knot made over two central filler cords using two working cords; forms the basic unit of most macramé fabric.
A pattern in which adjacent square knots share filler cords from neighbouring knots, creating an offset grid that produces an open, net-like fabric.
A simple mounting knot used to attach cords to a ring or bar; folds the cord at its midpoint and passes both ends through the loop.
The passive central cord(s) over which working cords are knotted; holds shape and tension.
The active cord used to tie knots over the filler; does most of the movement while the filler cord stays relatively stationary.

content.text: This circular rug starts from a 15 cm metal ring and grows outward using
alternating square knots
. Because a circle's circumference grows as the radius increases, more
working cords

must be added every 5 cm of radius to keep the piece lying flat. The process is methodical rather than technically difficult: the main skill is keeping consistent tension through a large number of cords. 8 mm braided cotton gives a dense, cushioned surface; natural undyed cotton works well, or use pre-dyed cord for a coloured rug.
Setup

You will need approximately 120 m of 8 mm braided cotton cord. Cut 16 cords each 5 m long for the starting round; you will cut additional cords of the same length as the rug grows. A large flat board on the floor is the best work surface for a piece this size.
Mount all 16 starting cords onto the 15 cm central ring using
lark's head knots
at their midpoints. You now have 32 working ends radiating outward from the ring. Arrange them evenly around the ring; each group of 4 adjacent cords will become one
square knot
unit.
Method
Step 1: Work the first round of square knots. Divide the 32 cords into 8 groups of 4. In each group, use the outer 2 as working cords and the inner 2 as
filler cords
. Tie one square knot per group, keeping it close to the centre ring. This gives you 8 square knots in the first round.

Step 2: Work the second round using alternating square knots. Take 2 cords from the right of one knot and 2 cords from the left of the next knot; tie a square knot between the groups. This creates the offset alternating pattern. Continue around the circle.

Step 3: After every 5 cm of radius (measured from the centre ring), add 4 new cords to the work. To add: fold a new 5 m cord at its midpoint and attach it to an existing cord using a lark's head knot, positioning it between two existing knot groups. The new cord's 4 ends join the adjacent knot groups and create an additional knot unit in the next round. The number of square knots per round should increase by approximately 4 every 5 cm.

Step 4: Keep working outward. Add cords every 5 cm and continue the alternating square knot rounds. Stop when the rug reaches 55 cm across. The edge finish will add the final 5 cm to reach the 60 cm target.

Step 5: Edge finish. On the outermost round, work one row of tight square knots using all remaining cords. Then bring all cord ends to the back of the rug, thread them through adjacent knots using a blunt darning needle to secure, and trim close to the knot surface. Alternatively, work a wrapped edge: gather all cord ends in groups of 4 and wrap each group tightly for 3-4 cm, then trim.
Troubleshooter

attrs.problem: Rug cups upward at the edges

content.text: Not enough cords were added as the radius grew; the outer rounds are too tight relative to the area they must cover. Add additional lark's head cords at the next round and work them in. Blocking the rug flat with water and pins will flatten minor cupping.

attrs.problem: Rug rucks and folds in the centre

content.text: Too many cords were added too quickly; the inner rounds are looser than the outer ones, creating excess fabric at the centre. This is difficult to reverse once several rounds are worked. For future rounds, add cords slightly later (every 6 cm rather than every 5 cm) and assess flatness after each addition.

attrs.problem: Knots are visible as a lumpy surface

content.text: This is expected with 8 mm cord. Press the finished rug firmly under a heavy flat object (a stack of books, a board) overnight while slightly damp to compress the knots and produce a flatter, more even surface.
